betterquesting:10.desc:8:
§6The Brain's Core: EEPROM & BIOS§f

§aProgram the boot-loader logic that initializes your system and loads your OS!§f
Every computer needs basic system firmware to start. This is handled by the §bEEPROM§f, which stores the bootstrap BIOS code.

§6EEPROM & BIOS Details§f:
§b• EEPROM (Lua BIOS)§f: A tiny memory chip containing the default bootloader script. By default, it looks for an attached disk drive containing an operating system (like OpenOS).
§b• Memory Constraints§f: The maximum size of the byte array that can be written to an EEPROM as configuration/program data is capped at §e4096 bytes (4 KB)§f.
§b• Lua Architecture§f: OpenComputers runs on §bLua 5.3§f. To write a custom BIOS, you must flash code that conforms to Lua API execution.

§6Writing & Erasing EEPROMs§f:
§b• EEPROM Writer§f: Place a §bBlank EEPROM§f in the §bEEPROM Writer§f block to write custom boot codes. Re-writing EEPROMs is balanced to be moderately expensive, drawing §e50 energy§f per write.
§b• Custom Labels§f: You can name your custom EEPROMs to keep track of different boot protocols.

§9Setting Boot Address§f:
If your computer has multiple drives attached, you can define which drive to boot from. Open the Lua shell, call §bcomputer.setBootAddress(address)§f to lock in a specific storage unit, and §bcomputer.getBootAddress()§f to verify it.
